required.title: [Activity] Organize and execute at least one group activity with a civic or service purpose.

suggested.author: Justin Roberts

competence_markers.description: The child has helped organize at least one group activity
They can explain how the group made decisions and what their role was
They can articulate a position in a group and listen to an opposing view without disruption
They understand that leadership is about helping the group succeed, not about being in charge

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you organized a group to do something.
narration_prompts.detail: How did you decide what to do? How did you get people to help? What happened?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a meeting you ran or helped run.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the purpose? How did you make sure everyone was heard?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you disagreed with someone in a group.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? How was it resolved?
It is not about being the boss — so what is it about?

narration_prompts.prompt: If you could organize one project in our community, what would it be?

cross_connections.description: This packet applies 4.3's leadership concepts in a civic context.
Service projects provide natural opportunities for group leadership.
Speaking up in groups requires the communication skills from 3.3.

cross_connections.target_name: Living as Part of a Community
cross_connections.description: Leadership experience is part of the capstone assessment.
Leading fairly and honestly is a character exercise.

content.why_this_matters:
Sometimes, getting something done requires more than one person. If you want to clean up a park, you need friends to help. If you want to change something at school, you need to talk to people and get them on your side. If your family is deciding where to go on vacation, everyone has an opinion and someone has to help the group make a decision.

This is what leadership looks like at its most basic: helping a group of people work together toward something they all want. It is not about being the boss. It is not about being the loudest. It is about being the person who helps everyone get organized, who listens to what people want, who finds a fair way to make decisions, and who makes sure things actually get done.

You are already a leader in ways you might not realize. Every time you organize a game on the playground, suggest a project, or help your family decide something, you are practicing leadership. This packet helps you do it better — and start doing it for things that matter to your community.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Group organizing** — the ability to coordinate people toward a shared purpose
2. **Basic meeting skills** — stating a purpose, hearing from others, making a decision
3. **Speaking up in groups** — articulating a position with clarity and respect
4. **Fairness and compromise** — working through disagreements toward outcomes the group can accept
5. **Responsibility for outcomes** — understanding what it means to be the person who makes sure something happens
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Getting People Together for a Purpose

Think of a time you wanted to do something that required more than just you. Maybe you wanted to play a game that needed teams. Maybe you wanted to build a big fort. Maybe you wanted to collect food for a food drive. In each case, you needed other people — and you needed to organize them.

Organizing people means answering a few simple questions:
- **What are we trying to do?** The goal needs to be clear. "Let us clean up the park" is better than "let us do something good."
- **Who is going to help?** You need people. Ask them. Explain the goal. Make them want to participate.
- **Who does what?** When everyone shows up, they need to know their job. "You collect the big trash, I will pick up the small stuff, and you hold the bags." Clear roles make work go faster and prevent arguments.
- **When are we doing it?** Pick a time. Tell everyone. Make sure people actually show up.

This is organizing. It is not glamorous. It is not complicated. But it is the foundation of everything from a neighborhood cleanup to a national movement. Every great achievement that involved more than one person started with someone answering these four questions.

### Part 2: Running a Simple Meeting

A meeting is just a group of people talking about something on purpose. That is all it is. But meetings can be great or terrible depending on how they are run.

A good meeting has three parts:

**The purpose.** Someone says why the group is together. "We are meeting to decide what our service project will be this month." Clear and simple.

**The discussion.** Everyone gets a chance to share their ideas. The person running the meeting makes sure everyone is heard — not just the loudest person. If someone has not spoken, ask them: "What do you think?" If two people disagree, let them both explain their view.

**The decision.** The group decides what to do. Sometimes everyone agrees. Sometimes you vote. Sometimes you compromise — taking parts of different ideas and combining them. The important thing is that the decision is clear and that everyone knows what happens next.

**Practice this.** Run a family meeting about something real — where to go on the weekend, what to have for a special dinner, how to organize the household chores. You state the purpose, you make sure everyone speaks, and you help the group reach a decision. That is facilitation, and you just did it.

### Part 3: Saying What You Think

In any group, there will be moments when you need to say what you think. This can feel scary. What if people disagree? What if they laugh? What if you are wrong?

Here is the truth: saying what you think — clearly, respectfully, and with a reason — is one of the most important skills you will ever develop. Here is how:

**State your idea.** "I think we should do X."
**Give your reason.** "Because Y."
**Listen to the response.** Other people may agree, disagree, or have a different idea. That is fine. The goal is not to win — the goal is to contribute your thinking to the group.

If someone disagrees with you:
- Listen to their reason. Maybe they have a point you had not considered.
- You do not have to change your mind. But you should be able to say: "I hear what you are saying, and I still think X because Y."
- If the group decides differently, accept the decision. Being part of a group means sometimes your idea does not win — and the group is still worth being part of.

### Part 4: When People Want Different Things

In any group, people will disagree. This is normal. This is healthy. The group that never disagrees is a group where some people are not speaking up.

When disagreement happens:
- **Listen to both sides.** Everyone who has an opinion should get to explain it.
- **Look for common ground.** Do the disagreeing sides have anything in common? Often, people want the same outcome but disagree about how to get there.
- **Consider compromise.** Can you combine the best parts of both ideas? Can you alternate — do one person's idea this time and the other's next time?
- **Sometimes someone is right.** Not every disagreement is a case for compromise. Sometimes one side has better reasons. If that is the case, the group should be able to recognize it and act accordingly.
- **Accept the outcome.** Once the group decides, support the decision even if it was not your first choice. A group that falls apart after every disagreement cannot accomplish anything.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Group Organizing
**Developing well:** The child has organized at least one group activity. They can describe how they got people together, what the goal was, and what happened. They show initiative in coordinating, not just participating.

**Developing slowly:** The child has not initiated any group organizing. They participate in groups but do not take initiative.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you provided an opportunity? Some children need a prompt: "Would you like to organize a park cleanup with your friends? I can help you figure out how."

### 2. Meeting Facilitation
**Developing well:** The child can run a simple meeting: state the purpose, invite input, help the group decide. Even if it is a family discussion about weekend plans, the child demonstrates the structure.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot structure a group discussion. Conversations are chaotic or dominated by one person.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled meeting facilitation? Children who have never seen a well-run meeting cannot run one. Model first, then invite them to try.

### 3. Speaking Up
**Developing well:** The child articulates positions in group settings with clarity. They give reasons for their views. They can disagree respectfully.

**Developing slowly:** The child is silent in groups or speaks without giving reasons.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the group environment safe for disagreement? Children who have been shut down for disagreeing will not speak up. Create an environment where "I think differently" is valued.
